MTH6139 Time Series

Analysing Stock Market Returns in Relation To Volatility

Aaron Tahiri

Spring term 2025

Section 1: Introduction

This report analyses financial time series data and uses Meta Prophet to forecast, in particular, We are exploring the the NASDAQ Index, and the VIX Volatility index

The NASDAQ represents the performance of technology based equities and some of the most significant stocks, which we are using to represent the general stock market, while the VIX measures expected market volatility. These two variables are closely related as we will see when we compare the 2 time series’

The aim of this project is to explore the correlations in behaviour between these time series and produce forecasts using Prophet.

Section 2: Data Collection

The data was obtained from Yahoo Finance using the quantmod package, which I used daily data of the NASDAQ and VIX from the year 2000 onwards thi time period captures multiple market regimes and events, such as the dot com crash, the financial crisis, and the COVID19 pandemic.

library(quantmod)
## Loading required package: xts
## Loading required package: zoo
## 
## Attaching package: 'zoo'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     as.Date, as.Date.numeric
## Loading required package: TTR
## Registered S3 method overwritten by 'quantmod':
##   method            from
##   as.zoo.data.frame zoo
getSymbols("^IXIC", src = "yahoo", from = "2000-01-01")
## [1] "IXIC"
getSymbols("^VIX", src = "yahoo", from = "2000-01-01")
## Warning: ^VIX contains missing values. Some functions will not work if objects
## contain missing values in the middle of the series. Consider using na.omit(),
## na.approx(), na.fill(), etc to remove or replace them.
## [1] "VIX"

Section 3: Data Preparation

The data needs to be in the correct format.

nasdaq <- data.frame(
  ds = index(IXIC),
  y = as.numeric(Cl(IXIC))
)

vix <- data.frame(
  ds = index(VIX),
  y = as.numeric(Cl(VIX))
)

nasdaq <- na.omit(nasdaq)
vix <- na.omit(vix)

Section 4: Exploratory Data Analysis

4.1 NASDAQ Time Series

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(nasdaq, aes(x = ds, y = y)) +
  geom_line(color = "blue") +
  labs(title = "NASDAQ Composite Index",
       x = "Date",
       y = "Index Value")

The NASDAQ shows a clear upward trend over time, with noticeable drops during major financial crises.

4.2 VIX Time Series

ggplot(vix, aes(x = ds, y = y)) +
  geom_line(color = "red") +
  labs(title = "VIX Volatility Index",
       x = "Date",
       y = "VIX Level")

The VIX exhibits sharp spikes during periods of market stress, indicating increased uncertainty and volatility.

4.3 Combined Plot

The scaled plot can show an inverse relationship between the NASDAQ and VIX. Periods of declining market performance tend to coincide with increases in volatility, reflecting higher market uncertainty. THis is wha can be seen when we look at major events.

Section 5: Forecasting NASDAQ with Prophet

library(prophet)
## Loading required package: Rcpp
## Loading required package: rlang
model_nasdaq <- prophet(nasdaq)
## Disabling daily seasonality. Run prophet with daily.seasonality=TRUE to override this.
future_nasdaq <- make_future_dataframe(model_nasdaq, periods = 365)
forecast_nasdaq <- predict(model_nasdaq, future_nasdaq)

plot(model_nasdaq, forecast_nasdaq)

The forecast shows that the NASDAQ maintains a long-term bullish trend, although short-term fluctuations remain uncertain.

Section 6: Forecasting VIX

model_vix <- prophet(vix)
## Disabling daily seasonality. Run prophet with daily.seasonality=TRUE to override this.
future_vix <- make_future_dataframe(model_vix, periods = 365)
forecast_vix <- predict(model_vix, future_vix)

plot(model_vix, forecast_vix)

While we tried to forecast the VIX, I conclude that it is more difficult to forecast or predict the VIX.

Section 7: Returns and Volatility Relationship

library(dplyr)
## 
## ######################### Warning from 'xts' package ##########################
## #                                                                             #
## # The dplyr lag() function breaks how base R's lag() function is supposed to  #
## # work, which breaks lag(my_xts). Calls to lag(my_xts) that you type or       #
## # source() into this session won't work correctly.                            #
## #                                                                             #
## # Use stats::lag() to make sure you're not using dplyr::lag(), or you can add #
## # conflictRules('dplyr', exclude = 'lag') to your .Rprofile to stop           #
## # dplyr from breaking base R's lag() function.                                #
## #                                                                             #
## # Code in packages is not affected. It's protected by R's namespace mechanism #
## # Set `options(xts.warn_dplyr_breaks_lag = FALSE)` to suppress this warning.  #
## #                                                                             #
## ###############################################################################
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:xts':
## 
##     first, last
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
nasdaq_returns <- nasdaq %>%
  arrange(ds) %>%
  mutate(return = c(NA, diff(log(y))))

merged_data <- nasdaq_returns %>%
  select(ds, return) %>%
  inner_join(vix, by = "ds")

colnames(merged_data) <- c("ds","return","vix")

Here we investigate potential relationships between returns and volatility

7.1 Returns Over Time

ggplot(merged_data, aes(x = ds, y = return)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(title = "NASDAQ Log Returns",
       x = "Date",
       y = "Return")
## Warning: Removed 1 row containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_line()`).

7.2 Returns vs VIX

ggplot(merged_data, aes(x = return, y = vix)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  labs(title = "NASDAQ Returns vs VIX",
       x = "Return",
       y = "VIX")
## Warning: Removed 1 row containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).

Now with the NASDAQ log returns, we can plot against the VIX, we can see that negative returns tend to be associated with higher volatility.

Section 8: Conclusion

This project applied Prophet to forecast financial time series data, by analysing the correlation between two financial time series

The NASDAQ shows strong long term growth, while the VIX shows periods of market stress through volatility spikes, their relationship and movements often coincide

Although Prophet effectively models long term trends, financial markets still remain difficult to predict in the short term due to their stochastic nature and the multitude of factors affecting it.

Further work could include additional variables or alternative forecasting methods.
